---
title: "Untitled"
author: "Jahred"
date: "2026-05-12"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## 

Let $Y_{gt}$ denote the observed count in the masked ZIP2 region $g$ at week $t$ and let $Y^*_{zt}$ denote the latent unobserved count in the compatible ZIP3 region $z$ forming $g$ with the constraint:

$$
 Y_{gt} = \Sigma_{z \ \epsilon \ g}Y^*_{gt}
$$

So we can model each:

$$
Y^*_{zt} \sim \text{Multinomial}(Y_{gt}, p_{zt})
$$

where $p_{zt}$ is informed by population size, baseline AGI rates, historical ZIP3 trends, flooding severity, seasonality, and perhaps neighboring ZIP3 behavior.

The intuition is that the observed ZIP2 total is fixed, while the true ZIP3 composition is only partially known. A population-only split is one special case of this model, with p_zt proportional to population. This is a bit more stronger because it lets the allocation probabilities borrow strength from geography and pre-event data, which is exactly the kind of structure hierarchical models are designed to capture.

I would be cautious, though, about using post-event outcomes directly in the allocation model, because that could leak treatment information into the imputation step. Ideally, the allocation probabilities would be estimated from pre-hurricane history and stable covariates, then used to distribute the masked counts in the post-period.

outcome for each week:

290: total possible unobserved values compatible with 290; sum of 29\* and 2\*\* at week t

291: total possible unobserved values compatible with 291; sum of 29\* and 2\*\* at week t

289: total possible unobserved values compatible with 289; sum of 28\* and 2\*\* at week t

288: total possible unobserved values compatible with 288; sum of 28\* and 2\*\* at week t
